summary:
The Siege of Baghdad (1258 AD) was one of the most catastrophic events in Islamic history, as the Mongol [[event:2306|Ilkhanate]] forces under Hulagu Khan — grandson of [[event:2225|Genghis Khan]] — captured, sacked, and burned the [[event:2134|Abbasid Caliphate's]] capital, killing the Caliph al-Musta'sim and ending the five-century-old institution of the Abbasid Caliphate that had been the symbolic center of Sunni Islam since 750 AD. The siege lasted less than two weeks (January 29 – February 10, 1258); the city's defenders, weakened by internal political conflicts and underestimating the Mongol threat, surrendered after token resistance.

The sack of Baghdad was conducted with characteristic Mongol thoroughness. The [[event:2136|House of Wisdom]] — the great repository of Islamic learning accumulated over centuries — was destroyed; it is said that the waters of the Tigris ran black with ink from thrown manuscripts and red with blood. Contemporary accounts describe 80,000 to 800,000 killed (modern estimates suggest the lower figures are more plausible). The caliph was executed, reportedly wrapped in rugs and trampled by horses to avoid shedding royal blood. The psychological impact on the Islamic world was immense: the institution of the caliphate — which however diminished in real power had served as a symbol of Islamic unity and legitimacy — was extinguished. The [[event:2275|Mamluk Sultanate]] of Egypt, which halted the Mongol advance at the [[event:2978|Battle of Ain Jalut]] (1260), became the new center of Sunni Islamic resistance, eventually hosting a refugee Abbasid caliph with purely ceremonial authority.
